shortDescription.text: Server-Side Template Injection via user-controlled Jinja2 Template instantiation

fullDescription.text: User-supplied input is passed directly to Jinja2 Template() constructor, allowing arbitrary template expression evaluation. Attackers can traverse __subclasses__ to reach os.system or subprocess and achieve Remote Code Execution.

shortDescription.text: Remote Code Execution via Server-Side Template Injection

fullDescription.text: The SSTI vulnerability in this endpoint allows attackers to execute arbitrary OS commands by traversing Python's object hierarchy through __class__.__mro__[1].__subclasses__() to locate subprocess.Popen or os.system.

shortDescription.text: Reflected XSS via |safe filter on SSTI output

fullDescription.text: The rendered template result is output with the |safe Jinja2 filter, bypassing HTML auto-escaping. Even without full RCE, an attacker can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript that executes in the victim's browser.

message.text: User-controlled 'template' query parameter is passed directly to jinja2.Template() constructor. The parameter flows from request.args.get('template') on line 241 to Template(template_input) on line 246 with zero sanitization. An attacker can supply payloads such as {{ ''.__class__.__mro__[1].__subclasses__() }} to enumerate all Python classes and escalate to RCE via subprocess.Popen or os.system.

snippet.text:
template_input = request.args.get("template", "Hello {{ name }}!")
    name = request.args.get("name", "World")
    try:
        from jinja2 import Template
        t = Template(template_input)
        result = t.render(name=name)

description.text: Do not allow user-controlled template strings. Use parameterized rendering with fixed templates. If dynamic templates are required, use jinja2.sandbox.SandboxedEnvironment which restricts attribute access and prevents __subclasses__ traversal.

message.text: The SSTI vulnerability at GET /ssti enables full Remote Code Execution. The Jinja2 Template is instantiated without a sandboxed environment, so an attacker can traverse Python MRO: {{ ''.__class__.__mro__[2].__subclasses__()[X]('id',shell=True,stdout=-1).communicate() }} where X is the index of subprocess.Popen. The application itself documents this payload in the rendered page at line 267.

snippet.text:
from jinja2 import Template
        t = Template(template_input)
        result = t.render(name=name)

message.text: The rendered SSTI output is inserted into the response HTML using the Jinja2 |safe filter at line 261, which disables auto-escaping. Even without RCE payloads, a template input like <script>alert(document.cookie)</script> will execute JavaScript in the victim's browser, enabling session hijacking.

description.text: Remove the |safe filter so Jinja2 auto-escaping sanitizes the rendered result before embedding it in the outer template.
Flask application containing the /ssti endpoint with SSTI/RCE/XSS vulnerabilities
